aws-cloudwatch.customerPainPoint: We set static thresholds for CloudWatch alarms but they're constantly firing during normal business cycles like daily traffic peaks and end-of-month batch jobs — alert fatigue is causing our team to ignore alarms.
aws-cloudwatch.pitchStatement: New Relic's ML-based anomaly detection learns your metric seasonality — daily, weekly, and event-driven patterns — and fires alerts only when behavior deviates from expected norms, dramatically reducing false positives while catching real anomalies your static thresholds miss.
aws-cloudwatch.discoveryQuestion: How much alert fatigue does your team experience from CloudWatch alarms — and do you have a sense of what percentage of alarms are false positives due to expected traffic patterns?
aws-cloudwatch.nrqlExample: SELECT average(aws.lambda.Errors) FROM Metric WHERE aws.region = 'us-east-1' FACET aws.lambda.FunctionName TIMESERIES 1 hour

aws-cloudwatch.title: Cross-Service Correlation (Lambda Errors → DynamoDB Throttles)
aws-cloudwatch.customerPainPoint: When Lambda functions start erroring, we don't know if the issue is in the function code or a downstream service — we check CloudWatch for Lambda, then separately for DynamoDB, then for SQS, wasting 30 minutes just correlating timestamps.
aws-cloudwatch.pitchStatement: New Relic correlates CloudWatch metrics across Lambda, DynamoDB, SQS, and other services in a single timeline view, so when Lambda errors spike you can immediately see if DynamoDB throttles or SQS delivery failures are the upstream cause — without switching dashboards.
aws-cloudwatch.discoveryQuestion: When you're investigating a Lambda error spike, how long does it typically take to determine whether a downstream AWS service like DynamoDB or SQS is contributing to the errors?
aws-cloudwatch.nrqlExample: SELECT sum(aws.lambda.Errors), sum(aws.dynamodb.UserErrors + aws.dynamodb.ThrottledRequests), sum(aws.sqs.NumberOfMessagesSent) FROM Metric TIMESERIES 5 minutes

aws-cloudwatch.title: Historical Metric Retention Beyond CloudWatch Limits
aws-cloudwatch.customerPainPoint: CloudWatch only retains high-resolution metrics for 3 hours and standard metrics for 15 months — we need to do year-over-year capacity planning and trend analysis but we run out of history when we need it most.
aws-cloudwatch.pitchStatement: New Relic retains all your CloudWatch metrics indefinitely at full resolution, so you can query year-over-year trends for capacity planning, compare this Black Friday to last year's, or analyze long-term infrastructure cost drivers without losing historical data.
aws-cloudwatch.discoveryQuestion: Have you ever needed to look at CloudWatch metric history for capacity planning or trend analysis and hit the retention limit — and how did that limitation affect your ability to plan?
aws-cloudwatch.nrqlExample: SELECT average(aws.ec2.CPUUtilization) FROM Metric FACET aws.ec2.InstanceId COMPARE WITH 1 year ago TIMESERIES 1 day SINCE 1 year ago

aws-xray.title: End-to-End Trace Visualization Across Lambda, ECS, and EC2
aws-xray.customerPainPoint: Our application spans Lambda functions, ECS containers, and EC2 instances and when something is slow, we can't follow a single request across all three environments — X-Ray gives us fragments but no complete picture.
aws-xray.pitchStatement: New Relic ingests X-Ray trace data and combines it with native APM instrumentation to stitch together complete end-to-end traces across Lambda, ECS, and EC2 in a single waterfall view — so you see every hop a request makes and where time is actually spent.
aws-xray.discoveryQuestion: Can you currently follow a single user request from the frontend all the way through Lambda, ECS, and EC2 in one unified trace view — or do you have gaps in your trace visibility?
aws-xray.nrqlExample: SELECT count(*), average(duration) FROM Span WHERE cloud.provider = 'aws' FACET service.name, name ORDER BY average(duration) DESC LIMIT 20

aws-xray.title: Service Map with Actual Latency Contribution Per Segment
aws-xray.customerPainPoint: We have an X-Ray service map but it just shows connections — it doesn't tell us which service segment is consuming the most time in a slow request, so optimization efforts are based on guesswork rather than data.
aws-xray.pitchStatement: New Relic's service map enriches X-Ray data with latency contribution per segment, showing you not just that ServiceA calls ServiceB, but that ServiceB accounts for 73% of total request latency — making optimization priorities obvious and measurable.
aws-xray.discoveryQuestion: On your current service map, can you immediately see which service is contributing the most latency to your slowest user-facing requests — without having to drill into individual traces?
aws-xray.nrqlExample: SELECT percentile(duration, 50, 95, 99), count(*) FROM Span FACET service.name WHERE parentId IS NOT NULL TIMESERIES 10 minutes

aws-xray.title: Error Propagation Path Identification
aws-xray.customerPainPoint: When our application returns a 500 error, we don't know where in the call chain the root error occurred — a downstream service error propagates up through five layers and by the time we see it, the original error context is lost.
aws-xray.pitchStatement: New Relic traces error propagation through your distributed system, identifying the originating service and error alongside every downstream service that propagated it — so your team immediately knows whether to fix the root cause service or add better error handling in an intermediate layer.
aws-xray.discoveryQuestion: When a user-facing error occurs in your distributed application, how quickly can you identify the originating service that caused the error versus the services that just propagated it?
aws-xray.nrqlExample: SELECT count(*) FROM Span WHERE error = true FACET service.name, error.message TIMESERIES 5 minutes

aws-xray.title: P99 Latency Drill-Down by Service Segment
aws-xray.customerPainPoint: Our p99 latency is twice our p50 and we don't know which specific service segment or operation is responsible for the outlier traces — we're trying to improve tail latency without knowing where to look.
aws-xray.pitchStatement: New Relic lets you filter X-Ray traces to the p99 latency bucket and compare segment durations against median traces, immediately revealing which service or operation behaves differently in slow requests — giving you a precise optimization target.
aws-xray.discoveryQuestion: Do you have the ability to isolate your slowest 1% of traces and compare them segment-by-segment against your median traces to identify which operation is responsible for tail latency?
aws-xray.nrqlExample: SELECT percentile(duration, 50, 90, 99) FROM Span WHERE service.name IS NOT NULL FACET service.name, name ORDER BY percentile(duration, 99) DESC LIMIT 10

aws-xray.title: Sampling Gap Detection and Trace Coverage Validation
aws-xray.customerPainPoint: X-Ray samples only a fraction of our traces and we have no visibility into what we're missing — we might be sampling away the exact slow or error traces that would explain our p99 latency and intermittent failures.
aws-xray.pitchStatement: New Relic's distributed tracing uses Infinite Tracing to capture 100% of traces and apply head-based sampling that prioritizes anomalous, slow, and error traces — ensuring the traces you most need for debugging are never sampled away.
aws-xray.discoveryQuestion: Are you concerned that X-Ray sampling is discarding the traces you most need — specifically the slow or error traces that would explain your tail latency and intermittent failures?
aws-xray.nrqlExample: SELECT count(*) AS 'Total Spans', uniqueCount(traceId) AS 'Unique Traces', percentage(count(*), WHERE error = true) AS 'Error Span %' FROM Span WHERE service.name IS NOT NULL TIMESERIES 1 hour
